coredata.dc:title: Color-tunable graphene quantum dots via one-step nitrogen-induced intramolecular charge transfer for in vivo/vitro aminotransferase fluorescence sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chenxi
Qi, Liang
Zhang, Shaobo
Wang, Xiao
Guo, Xuecheng
Liu, Hongsheng
Shi, Xizhi
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring aspartate aminotransferase (AST) has been demonstrated to be a sensitive sign of liver dysfunction and an essential method for determining the health status of the living body. The quantitative analysis of AST in vivo/vitro is still challenged by complicated processes, expensive equipment, and/or limited sensitivity. A colorimetric assay using multicolored fluorescent nanomaterials holds great promise in vivo/vitro sensing for visual and highly sensitive disease diagnosis. However, achieving a one-step synthesis of multicolored fluorescence (FL) GQDs probe presents challenges. Herein, we successfully designed novel multicolored GQDs with a high quantum yield (QY) of 60.7 % via a simple nitrogen-induced intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) approach. Leveraging the non-toxic and color-tunable properties of N-GQDs, we developed an innovative N-GQDs-based FL test paper for sensitive colorimetric on-site detection of AST, employing the FL colorimetric method based on the transamination reaction. The FL quenching intensity demonstrated an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of AST within the range of 2.0−60.0 U/L, achieving an impressively low limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.065 U/L. Moreover, the N-GQDs were utilized for AST in vivo detection and FL imaging in living zebrafish, further showcasing their potential in various applications such as liver disease diagnosis, assessment of myocardial damage, and monitoring of drug and toxic poisonings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolored graphene quantum dots
Intramolecular charge transfer
Biological imaging
Aspartate aminotransferase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantification of AST color-tunable sensing in vitro
Real sample assay for AST colorimetric detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of N-GQDs1–6 synthesis (A); Schematic diagram of N-GQDs probe for on-site detection of AST (B); N-GQDs detection applications for living imaging of zebrafish and cell imaging of HepG2 cell(C).

(A, D) TEM and (B, E) particle size distribution image of the N-GQDs1 and N-GQDs6, respectively; (C) XPS spectra of N-GQDs1 and (F) N-GQDs6; (J) FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs1–6. XPS high-resolution N 1 s spectra of (H) the N-GQDs1 and (I) N-GQDs6, respectively.
The absorption and emission spectra of N-GQD1–6 (A); Excitation wavelength of N-GQDs1–6 (B).

(A) Curves of FL quenching of GQDs (10 μg/mL) with AST solution (0−60.0 U/L); (B) linear correlation between FL intensity (F/F0-1) and AST concentration. The error bars were standard deviations obtained from three repeat experiments; (C) Images of test paper with N-GQDs after addition of various concentration AST (0−60.0 μM) under UV lamp irradiation; (D) Chromaticity diagram to indicate the range of fluorescent test papers’ color variation; DFT calculation results (molecular structure, HOMO, LUMO, and energy gap) of N-GQDs1–3 with different ratios of nitrogen doping including pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and amino N(E); Mechanism diagram of ICT between FL emission induced by nitrogen(F).

The scheme of the biological imaging and AST detection application based on ICT mechanism(A); The photos of test papers were taken under a UV lamp and after the addition of AST in real serum samples under UV lamp (B).

Laser confocal microscope image of a 3-day-old zebrafish which were incubated in N-GQDs3, and (A) FL, (B) merger and (C) bright images of the belly; (D) FL spectrum of N-GQDs3 (200 mg/L); zebrafishes were incubated in N-GQDs3 after adding 0.5 % ethanol with for 24 h, and (E) FL, (F) merger and (G) bright images of the belly; (H) FL spectrum of zebrafishes’ supernatant after 0.5 % of ethanol treatment.

head.ce:title: Color-tunable graphene quantum dots via one-step nitrogen-induced intramolecular charge transfer for in vivo/vitro aminotransferase fluorescence sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring aspartate aminotransferase (AST) has been demonstrated to be a sensitive sign of liver dysfunction and an essential method for determining the health status of the living body. The quantitative analysis of AST in vivo/vitro is still challenged by complicated processes, expensive equipment, and/or limited sensitivity. A colorimetric assay using multicolored fluorescent nanomaterials holds great promise in vivo/vitro sensing for visual and highly sensitive disease diagnosis. However, achieving a one-step synthesis of multicolored fluorescence (FL) GQDs probe presents challenges. Herein, we successfully designed novel multicolored GQDs with a high quantum yield (QY) of 60.7 % via a simple nitrogen-induced intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) approach. Leveraging the non-toxic and color-tunable properties of N-GQDs, we developed an innovative N-GQDs-based FL test paper for sensitive colorimetric on-site detection of AST, employing the FL colorimetric method based on the transamination reaction. The FL quenching intensity demonstrated an excellent linear relationship with the concentration of AST within the range of 2.0−60.0 U/L, achieving an impressively low limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.065 U/L. Moreover, the N-GQDs were utilized for AST in vivo detection and FL imaging in living zebrafish, further showcasing their potential in various applications such as liver disease diagnosis, assessment of myocardial damage, and monitoring of drug and toxic poisonings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Liver disease represents a significant public health burden in both high and low-income countries, causing over two million annual, global deaths [1]. Various optical methods, such as colorimetry, spectrophotometry, chemiluminescence, chromatography, and fluorescence are employed for the quantitative and semiquantitative determination of aminotransferase [2,3]. However, there is a lack of robust, easy-to-use, and more reliable methods for early diagnosis to manage and treat liver failures in low-income settings. Thus, the development of a novel, rapid, and easy-to-use method for the early diagnosis of liver disease in underdeveloped areas was urgent. Detection of aspartate aminotransferase (AST) levels provides more information as to the source of disease for early diagnosis of liver cancer.

To date, researchers have put significant effort into developing sensor applications for AST-level analysis in the context of hepatic injury, for instance, electrochemical, and fluorescence techniques [4]. The biosensor based on enzyme catalysis has excellent specificity and selectivity, and clear catalytic mechanism. However, the performance of enzymatic sensors is closely related to the stability and bioactivity of the redox enzyme that is part of the sensor, which is often strongly affected by environmental factors, including temperature and pH. To date, the enzyme-free fluorescence biosensors, have attracted increasing attention as they would not suffer from such drawbacks. Non-enzymatic catalysis biosensors usually use modified nano materials to add more active sites for target molecule sensing [5,6]. Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) or carbon dots (CDs) have gained widespread recognition as fluorescence enzyme-free probe in disease detection and biological imaging due to their exceptional adjustability in optical properties, remarkable biocompatibility, catalytic activity and low toxicity in mammalian systems [7,8]. So far, so many kinds of GQDs have been extensively modified and employed as single-emission fluorescence probes or sensors for the sensitive detection of various ions and small molecules [9–11]. However, it is important to note that these single-emission fluorescence probes come with inherent limitations, including local concentration of the fluorescent sensor, background interference, non-visualization and more. These limitations collectively impede their qualitative and quantitative capabilities in point-of-care testing. Multicolored fluorescent probes or sensors, typically comprising more than two fluorescence (FL) substances, including a target-sensitive FL dye and reference dyes [12], can be designed and developed using colorimetric methods. Nevertheless, multicolor GQDs were synthesized with complex process and color-tunable mechanism is not clear so that they are limited to apply in many sensing platforms [13–18].

Inspired by intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) mechanism, the color-adjustable properties of carbon dots (CDs) were first explored by Xu et al [8]. Specifically, the presence of a conjugated system in CDs comprising electron donors or acceptors of some functional groups is a fundamental prerequisite for the ICT [19,20]. This is primarily due to the complexities associated with their synthesis, suboptimal QY, and limited water solubility, which collectively impede their utility in bioimaging and biosensing applications. Consequently, there is a pressing need to develop hydrophilic GQDs endowed with color-tunable properties, high-efficiency luminescence, and convenient synthesis methods, as this prospect holds immense potential for advancing both research and applications in enzyme-free sensing and in vivo detection field.

In this work, we embarked on the design and synthesis of novel multicolored nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) based on nitrogen induced ICT mechanism to achieve AST in vivo/vitro sensing via increasing intramolecular pull-push electron CN. The observed redshift in the fluorescent emission centers was attributed to the presence of CN-based conjugated structures, as substantiated by XPS measurements, density functional theory (DFT) and a series of meticulously controlled experiments. By capitalizing on unique properties of N-GQDs, the N-GQDs-based test paper was successfully applied for sensitive AST non-enzyme catalytic via transition from -NH2 to CO during the transamination reaction, then the colorimetric detection of N-GQDs FL redshift was achieved via the increasing of relative ratio of CN/-NH2 in the surface state, meanwhile the ICT mechanism activation mechanism was confirmed. Exploiting their excellent aqueous solubility and biocompatibility, N-GQDs were further employed for intracellular and living body imaging. Notably, we successfully conducted fluorescence, highlighting the multifunctional and exceptional potential applications of N-GQDs in in vivo AST sensing and early diagnosis of liver disease.
The material and methods are summarized in the supplementary material.

Multicolor N-GQDs sensor were synthesized by nitrogen-induced intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) approach via one-step solvothermal method ( Fig. 1 A). According to the different ratios of citric acid and urea used (1:1, 1:2, 1:5 1:7, 1:13, and 1:15, w/w), N-GQDs samples were labeled as N-GQDs1, N-GQDs2, N-GQDs3, N-GQDs4, N-GQDs5, and N-GQDs6, respectively. Notably, the presence of nitrogen atoms within the N-GQDs proved instrumental in generating long-wavelength emissions. We anticipated that their concentration would influence the extent of sp2 hybridization, consequently resulting in a noticeable shift in coloration. This property holds significant promise for a range of applications, including point colorimetric detection with AST based on transamination reactions (Fig. 1 B), as well as the development of biological imaging (Fig. 1 C).

The multicolor N-GQDs was conducted using TEM, XRD, FT-IR NMR, Raman spectroscopy, and XPS to elucidate the underlying possible redshift mechanism. TEM examination, alongside particle size distribution analysis ( Figs. 2 A, 2 B, 2 D, 2 E), revealed that all samples had uniform particle sizes of approximately 3–4 nm (inset image), indicating these particles were well dispersed. Since these N-GQDs1–6 did not show significant changes in particle size, it suggested that the redshift phenomenon was not caused by the quantum size effect. HRTEM images (Fig. S1) exhibited discernible lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the plane lattice of the graphene pattern. XRD spectra (Fig. S2) showcased a prominent peak centered at ≈22.8°, along with two weaker peaks at ≈26.8° and 42.5°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) facets, displaying interlayer spacings of 0.10 and 0.11 nm, respectively. This indicated a relatively amorphous structure of the N-GQDs due to the presence of heteroatomic dopants or sp3 carbon defects in the graphitic framework [8,21]. Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S3) further confirmed the existence of such disordered and graphitic structures, clearly discerning the D-bands and G-bands of N-GQDs. In addition, FT-IR analysis in Fig. 2 J confirmed the existence of O- and N- containing functional groups in N-GQDs1–6 samples, characterized by the presence of CO (1682 cm−1), O-H (3204 cm−1), CC (1555 cm−1) and C-N (1208 cm−1) chemical bonds [22]. Notably, the stretching vibration intensity of N-H and CN increased gradually with higher nitrogen ratios. This outcome indicated the successful incorporation of nitrogen atoms in the GQDs. In C NMR spectrum (Fig. S4), signals from CN/CO groups at 168 ppm became more pronounced, signifying an augmented presence of CN/CO groups from GQDs1 to GQDs6. Moreover, the typical peaks ranging from 130–140 ppm were consistent with Ph-O groups (131.3 and 131.8 ppm), which could be attributed to proton resonances in the graphitized cores.

To further elucidate the elemental composition and bonding configurations, the surface groups of N-GQDs1–6 were scrutinized through XPS (Fig. S5 ). The distinct peaks observed at 285.1, 400.9, and 531.1 eV, unequivocally indicated the presence of C, N, and O in the N-GQDs, respectively in Table S2. Specially, the N 1 s band (Fig. S5a) exhibited distinct peaks at 400.2 eV and 399.2 eV, attributed to pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like N, affirming the presence of newly formed polyaromatic structures containing CN groups. As detailed in Table S4, the proportion of N-H groups decreased from 37 % (N-GQDs1) to 26 % (N-GQDs6), while the proportion of CN groups increased from 63 % (N-GQDs1) to 71 % (N-GQDs6). Furthermore, the O 1 s spectra (Fig. S7) revealed the presence of CO (531.3 eV) and C-O/C-N (533.2 eV) groups, respectively. Table S5 highlighted a significant increase in the proportion of CO groups in the O 1 s spectrum, rising from 84.75 % (N-GQDs1) to 92.43 % (N-GQDs6). Based on the above characterizations, the N-GQDs featured carbonized graphitic cores adorned with -O and -N surface functional groups. Given the pivotal role of CN and CO functional group in optical properties of N-GQDs [8], the introduction of nitrogen induced a shift in the content of O and N atoms within the graphitic core, consequently leading to a corresponding adjustment in the graphitized plane system.

The fluorescence spectrums of N-GQDs1–6 exhibited conspicuous redshifts correlated with increasing nitrogen content. Fig. 3 A illustrates robust UV–vis absorption across the 300–500 nm range. The absorption peaks at 348–432 nm closely matched the excitation spectra (365.8–430.0 nm) shown in Fig. 3 B, indicative of the redshift in FL emission center from 450 nm to 550 nm, attributable to the n-π* transition originating from CO, CN, and -NH2 [17,23,24]. Meanwhile, the small bond at ∼450 nm and large bond at ∼475 nm was corresponded to the fluorescence emission spectrum in Fig. 3 A, respectively. Nonetheless, the faint absorption peak in the high-energy region at 325 nm, attributed to the presence of CC in the aromatic skeleton, remained unchanged. Therefore, the absorption spectra (Fig. 3 A) vividly delineate the energy absorption centers stemming from both the CC carbon core and -NH2 surface states. Moreover, the PLQYs for the single fractions were 8.4 % for N-GQDs1, 22.2 % for N-GQDs2, 28.5 % for N-GQDs3, 30.3 % for N-GQDs4, 40.6 % for N-GQDs5 and 60.7 % for N-GQDs6 (Table S8). The presence of graphitic N in the sp2 domain of the carbon core allowed for an increase in the electron cloud density of the entire conjugated p-domain through the contribution of its two non-bonding electrons, ultimately increasing the PLQY [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of AST color-tunable sensing in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To precisely assess the sensing capabilities of N-GQDs, we employed them for the detection of AST. As shown in Figs. 4 A and 4 B, an excellent linear relationship was observed between the FL emission response and AST concentration (2.0–60.0 U/L), yielding a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9913). Remarkably, the limit of detection (LOD) plummeted to an impressively low 0.065 U/L (3σ/S).

Upon evenly applying an aqueous solution of AST onto the test paper fabricated with N-GQDs1, an obvious fluorescence variation proportional to the dosage of AST was discernible under UV light. As indicated in Fig. 4 C, varying dosages from 0 to 60.0 U/L corresponded to distinctive colors ranging from deep blue to light green, aligning seamlessly with the prior outcome (Fig. 4 A). The color coordinates of N-GQDs1 at different concentrations of AST (2.0–60.0 U/L) were plotted in Fig. 4 D, demonstrating a color range for the sensing platform that was consistent with the results obtained from the fluorescent test paper. Importantly, the blue fluorescence of the test paper at even 2.0 U/L of AST was lighter than that at 0 U/L but different from that (cyan) at 10.0 U/L, indicating the distinguished concentration as low as 2.0 U/L. Actually, the normal range for AST concentration in healthy individuals is between 10.0 to 40.0 U/L[3]. Therefore, the N-GQDs-based FL test paper showed the potential for early liver disease clinical diagnosis. Furthermore, to underscore the proficiency of N-GQDs1 as a FL sensor for AST detection, we compared it with various existing AST detection methods, the results of which are succinctly summarized in Table S10. Notably, the FL sensor exhibited heightened sensitivity compared to current techniques, boasting a lower LOD at 0.065 U/L and an extended linear range spanning from 2.0 to 60.0 U/L. These results affirm that the devised colorimetric FL probe and test paper exhibited remarkable efficiency, sensitivity, enabling swift, accurate, and quantitative AST detection in serum samples with exceptional visual clarity for a POC process.

The redshift mechanism of N-GQDs was further scrutinized via DFT calculations. Simulation and calculation of molecular model1–3 were built based on potential surface structures information of N-GQDs’ NMR results, incorporating pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and amine N groups, were constructed to investigate the orbital energy levels and the gaps between the HOMO and LUMO. As depicted in Fig. 4 E, the band gap for model1–3 of N-GQDs were −3.85 eV, −3.29 eV, and −3.28 eV, respectively. The narrower band gap indicated that electrons from the frontier orbitals of N-GQDs with graphitic nitrogen (CN) could transition to the next energy level with ease. Consequently, it was deduced that the augmentation of nitrogen content led to a reduction in the electronic gap. Concurrently, the prevalence of ICT states amplified in tandem with the augmentation of electron acceptors (CN) within the FL molecule. Grounded in the observed ICT phenomenon of N-GQDs, the FL redshift phenomenon was attributed to the introduction of CN as an electron acceptor [25]. Furthermore, Fig. 4 D offered a schematic diagram of the redshift mechanism. Initially, the states of the graphitized carbon core (CN) and surface state (-NH2) in N-GQDs likely played a pivotal role in shifting the FL emission spectrum. Specifically, the ratio of CN to N-H increased, resulting in the redshift of N-GQDs' emission spectra due to the electron-doping effect of nitrogen. Therefore, with an augmentation in the electron acceptor (CN), the ICT efficiency was bolstered, leading to a corresponding rise in the ICT component of the mixed excited state. Consequently, the heightened excited dipole moment led to a progressive redshift in the maximum absorption and FL spectrums of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample assay for AST colorimetric detection
ce:section.ce:para: The interference of other species found in the biological fluid was tested at their physiological concentrations to establish the specificity of the colorimetric assay of AST in biological samples. In Fig. S8A, the FL intensity of N-GQDs1 decreased slightly after adding different ions and molecules (Na+, K+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, glucose and ascorbic acid). Meanwhile, it was observed these ions and molecules except AST were not able to obviously change the FL intensity upon reacting with N-GQDs1. Furthermore, the N-GQDs showed relatively stable FL intensity at different pH values (5−10) in Fig. S8B. This result, beyond doubt, shown the selectivity of the method towards AST in vivo detection of cell and living body. Furthermore, to comprehensively assess the suitability of N-GQDs1 in real samples, we conducted AST concentration measurements in fetal bovine serum. As tabulated in Table S9, the recoveries of AST ranged between 92.0 % and 106.4 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) consistently below 6.0 %.

Fig. 5 A elucidated the redshift predicated on the ICT mechanism for the visual colorimetric detection of AST transaminases. During transamination, the -NH2 groups of N-GQDs1 underwent an exchange with another CO group catalyzed by AST. Then the obvious color variation was occurred in test paper, attributing to the increase ratio of intramolecular -NH2/CN on N-GQDs1. Leveraging this nitrogen-induced ICT mechanism of GQDs, the transammonia reaction was harnessed for the colorimetric detection of AST. As shown in Fig. 5 B, photographs of the fluorescent test paper were captured after adding bovine serum with varying AST concentrations. The fluorescent colors at 5.0 U/L, 10.0 U/L, 35.0 U/L, and 70.0 U/L demonstrated marked alterations. Consequently, the test paper sensor demonstrated its capability to promptly and distinctly ascertain whether serum AST levels were within the normal range or deviated, thus offering a potential foundation for the early diagnosis of hepatitis.

To extend the potential biological applications of N-GQDs as in vivo FL sensor to achieve early liver disease diagnosis and clinical prevention, we conducted an MTT assay to evaluate the viability of HepG2 cells following co-incubation with N-GQDs5 at concentrations ranging from 6.25 to 200.00 mg/L. As shown in Fig. S9, there was no significant decrease in cell viability after exposure to N-GQDs for 24 or 48 h. Even when subjected to a high concentration of N-GQDs5 (200 mg/L), the cell viability remained above 90 %, underscoring the non-toxic nature of N-GQDs5 towards the cells. This result substantiates the biocompatibility of N-GQDs5. Furthermore, we investigated the capability of N-GQDs for fluorescent biological imaging in HepG2 cells using CLSM [26]. As demonstrated in Fig. S10A, there was no discernible fluorescent signal after co-incubation with N-GQDs2 for 1 h. However, the fluorescent signal intensified with prolonged co-incubation, becoming notably stronger after 4 h. In addition, intercellular imaging was performed by introducing various concentrations of N-GQDs5, as depicted in Fig. S10B. The FL intensity displayed an upward trend in tandem with increasing concentrations of N-GQDs5, affirming their potential as highly effective intracellular detection probes.

Due to the high degree of genetic similarity between zebrafish and humans, zebrafish was selected as an in vivo model to explore the potential utility of N-GQDs3 as a fluorescent probe under standard culturing conditions due to the optimal excitation wavelength of N-GQDs3 (∼400 nm) is consistent with that of laser excitation (∼405 nm). Figs. S11A, 6 B, and 6 C depicted the entire body, head, and fishtail of the zebrafish, respectively, underscoring their appropriateness for laser confocal imaging. As illustrated in Figs. 6 A, 6 B, and 6 C, the entire stomach and spine of the 3-day-old zebrafish exhibited green FL, suggesting that N-GQDs3 could be distributed throughout the entire body of zebrafish through both swallowing and skin absorption. Comparing with blank groups, in Figs. 6 E, 6 F, and 6 G, the fluorescent image of zebrafish exhibited obvious redshift with yellow emission under the same excitation wavelength after ethanol treatment (0.5 % in water, v/v). This fluorescence redshift was attributed to liver toxicity induced by the zebrafish's incubation in an ethanol-containing solution for 24 h, resulting in elevated AST levels in the fish's belly [27]. Furthermore, the supernatant of zebrafishes’ body was measurement after ethanol (0.5 %) treatment. FL spectrum was significantly redshifted from 490 nm (Fig. 6 D) to 518 nm (Fig. 6 H). Due to liver cells was damaged via high-concentration ethanol treatment, AST in liver cells was released into the bloodstream. These results proved that N-GQDs hold considerable promise as an FL probe for biological imaging and in vivo AST determination.

In summary, we propose a straightforward one-step nitrogen-induced approach for the synthesis of multicolored N-GQDs based on the ICT mechanism for AST ultra-sensitive FL detection. Through meticulous data analysis, the AST catalysis transamination reaction was initiated through N-GQDs sensor due to an increased ratio of CN to -NH2 groups led to a progressively heightened push-pull electron effect, showing higher selectivity and sensitivity toward concentration of AST via colorimetric method. In addition, the MTT and living imaging of zebrafish results affirmed the biosafety of N-GQDs, while cell imaging revealed their potential for intracellular detection and biological imaging. Furthermore, we confirmed that colorimetric signal of N-GQDs were ultra-sensitively identified AST level in HepG2 cells and living zebrafish, demonstrating that N-GQDs hold tremendous promise for early diagnosis of liver cancer in vivo. Overall, this non-enzymic catalytic approach to ultra-sensitive detection will lead to a new research field at the interface of nanotechnology and in vivo sensing.

Liang Qi: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Chenxi Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Xiao Wang: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Shaobo Zhang: Validation, Investigation. Hongsheng Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xuecheng Guo: Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xizhi Shi: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge research funds Natural Science Foundation of China (32272340, 22178124), the 111 Project (B17018), Science and Technology Plan Project of Guangzhou (202102080150).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136245.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A quantitative electrochemical assay for liver injury

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Moed, M.H. Zaman, A quantitative electrochemical assay for liver injury, Biosens. Bioelectron., 131(2019) 74-78.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a paper-based analytical device for the colourimetric detection of alanine transaminase and the application of deep learning for image analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.E. Resmi, S. Sachin Kumar, D. Alageswari, P.V. Suneesh, T. Ramachandran, B.G. Nair, et al., Development of a paper-based analytical device for the colourimetric detection of alanine transaminase and the application of deep learning for image analysis, Anal. Chim. Acta, 1188(2021) 339158.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Current advancement in biosensing techniques for determination of alanine aminotransferase and aspartate aminotransferase-a mini review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Yadav, R. Jangra, B.R. Sharma, M. Sharma, Current advancement in biosensing techniques for determination of alanine aminotransferase and aspartate aminotransferase-a mini review, Process Biochem., 114(2022) 71-76.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alanine aminotransferase detection using TIT assisted four tapered fiber structure-based LSPR sensor: from healthcare to marine life

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chenxi Liu, Female, Doctor, School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China; Major: Food safety
Liang Qi, Male, Postdoctoral, School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China; Major: Food science

Shaobo Zhang, Male, Doctor, Centre for Nutrition and Food Sciences, Queensland Alliance for Agriculture and Food Innovation, University of Queensland, St Lucia, Brisbane, Queensland 4072, Australia; Food science
Xiao Wang, Female, Master, School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China; Major: Food safety

Xuecheng Guo, Male, Master, State Key Laboratory of Luminescent Materials and Devices Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Luminescence from Molecular Aggregates Center for Aggregation-Induced Emission South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China; Major: Materials Science and Engineering
Hongsheng Liu, Male, Professor, School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou, China; Major: Food science
Xizhi Shi, Male, Professor, Major: Food safety
